annotations.label: Abstract algebra

anchor.snippet: abstract algebra or modern algebra is the study of algebraic structures

annotations.note: Abstract algebra as a field of study is not itself a formal object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universal algebra

anchor.snippet: Universal algebra is a related subject that studies types of algebraic structures as single objects

annotations.note: Mathlib has no universal-algebra framework (signatures/varieties as single objects).

annotations.label: Variety of groups

anchor.snippet: which is called the variety of groups

annotations.note: No formalization of varieties in the universal-algebra sense exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rules of symbolical algebra

anchor.snippet: in symbolical algebra all rules of operations hold with no restrictions

annotations.note: Peacock's principle of permanence is a historical/philosophical statement with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Square-root identity over reals vs complex numbers

anchor.snippet: holds for the nonnegative real numbers , but not for general complex numbers

annotations.note: `Real.sqrt_mul` proves √(xy)=√x·√y for nonnegative reals; the complex failure is not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Monoid (Cayley)

anchor.snippet: defined a group as a set with an associative composition operation and the identity 1, today called a monoid

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Monoid` is exactly a set with an associative operation and an identity.

annotations.label: Kronecker's abstract binary operation

anchor.snippet: defined an abstract binary operation that was closed, commutative, associative, and had the left cancellation property

annotations.note: Mathlib has commutative left-cancellative monoids (`CommMonoid`/`LeftCancelMonoid`) but no single decl packaging exactly these historical axioms.

annotations.label: Weber's definition of a group

anchor.snippet: definition of a group was a closed binary operation that was associative and had left and right cancellation

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a finite (left/right) cancellative monoid is a group via `LeftCancelMonoid.groupOfFinite`, capturing Weber's finite-case characterization rather than a definition.

annotations.label: Sylow's theorem

anchor.snippet: Sylow's theorem was reproven by Frobenius

annotations.note: Sylow's theorems are formalized in `Mathlib.GroupTheory.Sylow`, including a generalization of Sylow I.

annotations.label: Cayley's theorem (embedding in permutation groups)

anchor.snippet: every finite group is a subgroup of a permutation group

annotations.note: `Equiv.Perm.subgroupOfMulAction` is Cayley's theorem, embedding any group (not just finite) into a symmetric group.

annotations.label: Jordan–Hölder theorem

anchor.snippet: He also completed the Jordan–Hölder theorem

annotations.note: `CompositionSeries.jordan_holder` proves uniqueness of composition factors for any Jordan–Hölder lattice (subgroups being one instance).

annotations.label: Associative algebra (Peirce)

anchor.snippet: gave an explicit definition of an associative algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Algebra R A` (with `Semiring A`) is precisely an associative unital algebra over a commutative ring.

annotations.label: Every algebra contains a nilpotent or idempotent element

anchor.snippet: proved that any algebra contains one or the other

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem states that every (finite-dimensional) algebra contains a nonzero nilpotent or idempotent element.

annotations.label: Frobenius theorem (real division algebras)

anchor.snippet: were the real numbers, the complex numbers, and the quaternions

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Frobenius's classification of finite-dimensional real associative division algebras as ℝ, ℂ, ℍ.

annotations.label: Decomposition and classification of simple Lie algebras

anchor.snippet: semisimple Lie algebras could be decomposed into simple ones, and classified all simple Lie algebras

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes that a semisimple Lie algebra decomposes uniquely into simple-ideal atoms, but the full Cartan–Killing classification of simple Lie algebras is not done.

annotations.label: Wedderburn decomposition of associative algebras

anchor.snippet: uniquely decomposes into the direct sums of a nilpotent algebra and a semisimple algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Wedderburn–Artin structure theorem for semisimple rings, but not the Wedderburn principal theorem (radical + semisimple decomposition of an algebra).

annotations.label: Gaussian integers form a UFD

anchor.snippet: showed that they form a unique factorization domain

annotations.note: ℤ[i] is given a `EuclideanDomain` instance in `GaussianInt`, so it is a UFD via Mathlib's EuclideanDomain → PID → UniqueFactorizationMonoid chain.

annotations.label: Unique factorization of ideals (Dedekind)

anchor.snippet: every nonzero ideal in the domain of integers of an algebraic number field is a unique product of prime ideals

annotations.note: `Ideal.uniqueFactorizationMonoid` gives unique factorization of ideals in any Dedekind domain, of which a number field's ring of integers is a special case.

annotations.label: Lasker–Noether theorem

anchor.snippet: every ideal in a polynomial ring is a finite intersection of primary ideals

annotations.note: `Submodule.isLasker` shows every Noetherian module admits a primary decomposition, generalizing the Lasker–Noether theorem for polynomial rings.

annotations.label: Gordan's finiteness theorem for invariants

anchor.snippet: the graded algebra of invariants of a binary form over the complex numbers was finitely generated

annotations.note: No Mathlib result on finite generation of the invariant algebra of a binary form.

annotations.label: Hilbert's basis theorem

anchor.snippet: any form of any degree or number of variables has a basis

annotations.note: `Polynomial.isNoetherianRing` is the Hilbert basis theorem: a polynomial ring over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian.

annotations.label: Fraenkel's axiomatic ring

anchor.snippet: a set with two operations addition, which forms a group

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Ring` (an additive group with a compatible associative multiplication) realizes this axiomatic notion of a ring.

annotations.label: Field (Dedekind's Körper)

anchor.snippet: for a set of real or complex numbers that is closed under the four arithmetic operations

annotations.note: Mathlib's abstract `Field` generalizes Dedekind's concrete subfields of ℂ closed under the four operations.

annotations.label: Domain of rationality (Kronecker)

anchor.snippet: defined what he called a domain of rationality

annotations.note: Kronecker's domain of rationality (a field generated by adjoining quantities) corresponds to Mathlib's `IntermediateField`/`Subfield` constructions.

annotations.label: Abstract field (Steinitz)

anchor.snippet: He axiomatically defined fields with the modern definition

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Field` is the modern abstract axiomatic definition Steinitz introduced.

annotations.label: Magma

anchor.snippet: Those sets that have a certain binary operation defined on them form magmas

annotations.note: Mathlib represents magmas via the `Mul` typeclass (a type with one binary operation), bundled as the category `MagmaCat`.

annotations.label: Structures with a single binary operation

anchor.snippet: Examples of algebraic structures with a single binary operation are

annotations.note: Single-operation structures (semigroup, monoid, group, etc.) are all formalized as typeclasses in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Structures with several operations

anchor.snippet: Examples involving several operations include

annotations.note: Multi-operation structures (rings, fields, modules, algebras) are all formalized as typeclasses in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group

anchor.snippet: A group is a set

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Group` class is the standard group definition.

annotations.label: Ring

anchor.snippet: A ring is a set

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Ring` class is the standard ring definition.

annotations.label: Poincaré conjecture

anchor.snippet: the fundamental group of a manifold, which encodes information about connectedness, can be used to determine whether a manifold is a sphere or not

annotations.note: The Poincaré conjecture is only *stated* (as `proof_wanted` declarations) in Mathlib, with no proof.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem (Wiles)

anchor.snippet: Andrew Wiles proved Fermat's Last Theorem

annotations.note: `FermatLastTheorem` is defined as a Prop and only special cases (e.g. n=4) and reductions are proven; Wiles's full proof is not in Mathlib.
